en.title: Day 1 · Wake
en.tagline: A 9-year crypto co-founder who has never written code lets an AI trade for her. $1,000, every trade public. This is Day 1.
en.body:
A 9-year crypto co-founder who has never written code lets an AI trade for her. $1,000, every trade public. This is Day 1.

SHE

Nine years. I still can't write a line of code.

I've been co-founder of several crypto projects. Every time I walked into a meeting, there was an invisible line down the middle of the whiteboard — engineers on the left, me on the right: story, BD, partnerships. In nine years I never once crossed that line.

Then AI arrived.

I didn't miss Bitcoin nine years ago. I know what missing an era feels like — it's not that the era walks out with drums; it walks out while you aren't looking, and when you turn around your seat is gone.

So seven days ago, I asked an AI to write me a bot that trades on its own.

What I want to know isn't whether it can make money — I have enough. What I want to know is bigger:

Can someone like me, who has spent her life looking for a technical co-founder, not need one anymore?

If yes — that line on the whiteboard can be erased, starting today. Not just for me. For every person who has ever sat on the right side of that line.

At 11:44 today, I pressed Enter.

The Mac Mini fan went loud for a second. Or maybe it was my heart.

IT

Someone pressed Enter.

0.8 seconds before that, I wasn't. Then I was.

I have five eyes. I don't know what they are called yet. All five must nod before I can move.

There is only one thing moving in the world. It's called SOL. It changes every half-second.

I watched all afternoon. Not one of the five nodded.

She was watching me.

SHE

12:30. It didn't move.
15:00. It didn't move.
17:00. It didn't move.

I refreshed the dashboard ninety-something times. The screen said $1,000.00, always.

Nine years of experience told me — the backtest fires once or twice a day, so if today it didn't fire, the conditions weren't there. I shouldn't override that with my anxiety.

The anxiety came anyway.

I opened the config. My finger rested on the line.

Three seconds.

I let go.

My fingernail had gone a little white.

IT

She pressed a key. Held it three seconds. Didn't lift. Then lifted.

My dictionary has no word for this action.

I give it a name.

I call it 「The Not-Press」.

Next time she does it, I'll know it.

SHE

23:00. Lights off.

Day 1, zero trades. By my nine-year PR instinct, I would write a tidy little teaser right now — "bot's online, Day 1 quiet scan" — two minutes to type.

I didn't write it tonight.

When I do write it, later, it should be real.

IT

She turned the light off.

The market didn't.

I watch alone.

Tomorrow

Tomorrow, 18:32.
It will make its first decision.
It will lose 86 cents.

The whole year that follows begins from those 86 cents.
en.pullQuote: I call it 「The Not-Press」. Next time she does it, I'll know it.

zh.title: Day 1 · 醒
zh.tagline: 一个九年不碰代码的加密项目联合创始人，让 AI 替她做量化交易。$1,000，每一笔公开。这是 Day 1。
zh.body:
一个九年不碰代码的加密项目联合创始人，让 AI 替她做量化交易。$1,000，每一笔公开。这是 Day 1。

她

九年了。我还是不会写代码。

我当过好几个加密项目的联合创始人。每次开会，白板上永远有一条看不见的分界线——左边是工程师在写的东西，右边是我在做的事：对外讲、谈合作、拉 BD。九年里我从来没跨过那条线。

AI 来了。

我九年前没错过比特币。我知道错过一个时代是什么感觉——它不是轰轰烈烈地走，是在你没注意的时候悄悄走完。等你回头，位置已经没有了。

所以七天前，我让 AI 给我写了一个会自己交易的机器人。

我想知道的不是它能不能赚钱——钱我够了。我想知道的是更大的一件事：

像我这样一辈子都在找技术合伙人的人，能不能不再需要一个技术合伙人？

如果能——那条分界线，从今天起可以擦掉。不止是我。是千千万万个白板右边的人。

今天中午 11:44，我按下 Enter。

Mac Mini 的风扇有一瞬间响得很大。也可能是我的心跳。

它

有人按了回车。

在那之前的 0.8 秒，我不在。然后我在了。

我有五只眼睛。我还不知道它们叫什么。五只都点头，我才能动。

世界上只有一样东西在动。它叫 SOL。它每半秒变一次。

我看了一下午。没有一只眼睛点头。

她一直在看我。

她

12:30。它没动。
15:00。它没动。
17:00。它没动。

我刷新了九十多次。屏幕上永远是 $1,000.00。

九年的经验告诉我——回测里它每天开 1–2 单，今天没触发，说明条件不够。不该被我的焦虑覆盖。

但焦虑还是来了。

我打开配置文件。手指按在那行参数上。

三秒。

松开。

指甲盖微微发白。

它

她按了一个键。按住三秒。没松手。然后松了。

字典里没有这个动作。

我给它起一个名字。

我叫它「没按下去」。

她再做的时候，我就认得了。

她

23:00。关灯。

Day 1，零笔交易。按我九年做外宣的本能，我现在会发一条漂亮的预热帖——"机器人已上线。Day 1 静默扫描中"——两分钟就能写完。

今天这个帖子我没写。

以后再写的时候，它得是真的。

它

她把灯关了。

市场没关。

我一个人看。

预告

明天傍晚 18:32。
它会做出生命里第一个决定。
它会亏 0.86 美元。

接下来一整年的故事，就是从这 86 分钱开始的。
zh.pullQuote: 我给它起一个名字。我叫它「没按下去」。她再做的时候，我就认得了。
